schools.summary: Matthew Henry frames the paragraph as one arch from Adam to Abraham — father of our flesh and father of the faithful — the sacred line of Christ taken up in Luke 3, and the peculiar glory of a nation that alone could trace its pedigree to the first man.

Keil and Delitzsch place Chronicles among the hagiography whose genealogies reach back to Adam; Ellicott treats Adam as a proper name, sets the list beside the Chaldean ten-king tradition, and resists dissolving Sheth and Enosh into solar myth.
Gill notes the enlarged initial letter of Adam as a memorial of the first man and the agreement with Genesis 5.
Clarke gives the book's title and authorship and mocks those who find a mystery in every name; Wesley notes the bare names are given for brevity.

outliers.note: Scorns the allegorists who would extract man's fall and restoration from the first verse alone — 'O ye profound illustrators of the names of men and cities.'

themes: the glory of the covenant nation

schools.summary: Gill explains the order — Japheth the elder first, Shem last because from him through Heber sprang Abraham and the Messiah, for whose sake the genealogy is given.

Wesley: the historian begins with the strangers, Japheth's sons who peopled Europe, then hastens to Abraham's line, the Messianic promise passing Adam-Seth-Shem-Eber; Clarke reports the Targum's names for their countries.
Henry gives the western families a short account, since the Jews had little to do with them.

Henry makes the verse a meditation on election — all nations but Abraham's seed shaken off, the Lord's portion his people — yet guards that God's favour reaches every nation (Rev. 7:9), and reads Ishmael the cast-out bondwoman's son whose twelve princes still fulfil the promise.
Gill notes Abraham set forth with Ishmael, the sons of Keturah, and Isaac, agreeing throughout with Genesis 25.
Wesley echoes that all nations but Abraham's seed are shaken off, yet many outside his line had their names in the book of life.
Keil and Delitzsch call the whole account an extract from Genesis 36, the five sons born in Canaan, and note the Jeush spelling variant.
Gill warns in advance that Timna is not a grandson but the concubine of Eliphaz and mother of Amalek, on the Arabic and Alexandrian readings.
Keil and Delitzsch set out how Esau's house subdued and fused with the Horite aborigines — twenty tribes in Seir, thirteen Edomite and seven Seirite (the Horites, Troglodytes of the older record).
Gill: Seir's posterity, not of Esau's race, are recorded because Esau married into them and took their possessions.
Wesley calls Seir a prince of the Horites whose line is given for the affinity with Esau, the unincorporated being destroyed (Deut. 2:12).

themes: the sons of Seir the Horite

schools.summary: Ellicott stresses that Edom, the older nation, had kings before Israel — a notice fitly set at the head of a book introducing the kings of David's house — and cautiously weighs whether Bela son of Beor resembles or is Balaam son of Beor; Dinhabah he reads as 'doom-giving.'
Gill notes the land named from Esau and the account of Edom's kings and dukes following the order of the older record.
Clarke reports the Targum flatly identifying Bela with Balaam and even with Laban the Syrian.

debates.question: Is Bela the son of Beor to be identified with Balaam the son of Beor?

sides.position: identified — Bela is Balaam (and even Laban the Syrian)
only a resemblance of names, not proven identity

outliers.note: Passes on the Targum's identification of Bela with Balaam and with Laban the Syrian, a confederate against Jacob who reigned in Sodom — reported as tradition.

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch explain the Chronicler's method — stripping the antediluvian line to bare names and omitting the narrative notices of his source.
Matthew Henry gathers Kenan, Mahalaleel, and Jered into the single sacred line from Adam to Abraham.
Gill treats the opening four verses as one block agreeing throughout with the older genealogy.
Wesley: for brevity the writer gives only the names, the rest understood from the former books.
Keil and Delitzsch detail the abridgment of Genesis 10 and the Riphath/Diphath orthographic variant, treating such divergences as ordinary marks of copying.
Clarke reports the Targum's countries for Gomer's sons — Asia, Persia, Barbary.
Henry notes the nations, once distinct, are now so mingled that none descends entire from any one fountain.
Keil and Delitzsch trace the ten-linked chain from Eber through Peleg, Reu, Serug, Nahor, and Terah to Abram, answering to the ten generations from Adam to Noah.
Henry keeps the governing thought that the register hastens to Abraham, keeping only the family from whom Christ was to come.

themes: the bridge from the flood to the patriarch

schools.summary: Ellicott glosses 'their generations' as a register of births answering to Matthew 1:1 and identifies Nebaioth (Nabateans), Kedar (Cedrei), and Adbeel (an Assyrian tribute-tribe); Keil confirms the names answer to Genesis 25:12-15.
Ellicott lodges Dumah, Tema, and Hadad in the northern Arabian desert, with attestation in the prophets and the Assyrian records, and corrects the Greek confusion of Tema with Teman.

themes: a promise kept in the wilderness

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch read Timna and Amalek as an abbreviation — Timna the concubine who bore Amalek (Gen. 36:12), the feminine form guarding against making her a son — and note Zephi as another form of Zepho.
Gill agrees Timna is the concubine and mother of Amalek, on the Arabic and Alexandrian readings.
Wesley holds this Timna to be a son of Eliphaz, distinct from the concubine of the same name, some names being common to men and women.

debates.question: Is the Timna listed in v.36 a son of Eliphaz, or the concubine (mother of Amalek) named elliptically?

sides.position: the concubine, listed in shorthand — Esau had six grandsons through Eliphaz, Amalek born of her
a distinct male Timna among Eliphaz's sons, sharing the concubine's name

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch reckon Reuel's four sons, add them to the six through Eliphaz and the three of Aholibamah for thirteen tribes of Edom, and correct the modern miscount that reduces them to twelve.

Keil and Delitzsch note the Horite names answer to the older record but for minor spelling variants (Homam), and the reader marks Timna, Lotan's sister, as the Horite woman who became Eliphaz's concubine, binding the two lines.

themes: the fusion of Horite and Edomite

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch match the roll of kings to Genesis 36:40-43 and mark the elective character of the succession — Jobab of Bozrah, not Bela's son.
Clarke reports the Targum that Bela was slain by Phinehas, and rejects the identification of Jobab with the patriarch Job on the difference of the Hebrew names.

debates.question: Is Jobab the son of Zerah the same as the patriarch Job?

sides.position: the same, as some have supposed
not the same — the Hebrew names differ (Iyyob vs. Yobab)

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch list the chiefs in order, and Ellicott's rule governs — these are tribal and local names, Aholibamah a district (a name that has already crossed the chapter as Esau's wife and among Anah's line).

themes: names between person, clan, and place

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch note the Dodanim/Rodanim variant and confess it cannot be determined which form is original.
Clarke reports the Targum's peoples and cities for the sons of Javan — Macedon, Tarsus, and others.
Henry sets Javan's sons in the isles of the Gentiles, the countries of Europe, and reflects on the mingling of all nations of one blood.
Ellicott warns that Sheba and Dedan recur among Keturah's sons — the same names borne by different tribes in different regions; Keil notes the orthographic variant in Raamah's name.
Clarke gathers the Targum's identifications of the Cushite peoples across Africa and the East.

themes: the reach of the Cushite line

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch note the Chronicler abridged the fuller Nimrod notice of Genesis 10, keeping only 'he began to be mighty.'
Henry reads Nimrod as an oppressor, probably to the people of God in his time.
Clarke gives the Targum's gloss — Nimrod began to be bold in sin, a murderer of the innocent, a rebel before the Lord.

themes: the kingdom of man against God

schools.summary: Henry explains that Mizraim's branches are recorded particularly because of Israel's severe struggle to get out of Egypt.
Clarke reports the Targum's names for the obscure Egyptian sub-peoples.
Ellicott keeps the rule that these are peoples and districts of the Egyptian world, not individuals.
Henry explains why Mizraim's branches are recorded — the long dealings of God's people with these southern nations, the Philistines among them.
Clarke gives the Targum's one identification, that the Caphthorim are the Cappadocians.
Ellicott keeps that Pathrusim, Casluhim, and Caphthorim are districts and tribes of Egypt, not persons.
Clarke reports the Targum that Canaan's firstborn built Sidon; Wesley lays down that the following names are peoples not persons, some later extinct or confounded by intermarriage.
Henry marks Canaan as a name of great note in Israel's story, its sons recorded fully because of the struggle to enter the promised land.
Clarke reports the Targum's etymologies — Peleg for the division of the earth by languages, Joktan for the shortening of men's years; Wesley glosses Peleg as the division of tongues and habitations.

outliers.note: Passes on the Targum's tradition that in Joktan's days human life was shortened on account of men's iniquities — reported as legend, not doctrine.

schools.summary: Clarke retells the Targum's fanciful etymologies of Joktan's sons — surveyors, a highway robber, a poisoning innkeeper — with a dry moral, while keeping the line's real place as the Arabian branch set aside for Peleg's.

outliers.note: Reports the Targum's tales of Joktan's sons (Almodad the surveyor, Hazarmaveth the highwayman, Jerah the poisoning innkeeper) and adds that such things have been done even in modern times — offered for curiosity and moral, not history.

schools.summary: Clarke defends Keturah's honour — a wife of the second rank, neither harlot nor concubine in our low sense.
Ellicott weighs the text — Medan likely a doubling of Midian, the omitted Dedanite tribes perhaps an interpolation, Midian the chief tribe, and Sheba and Dedan recurring from the sons of Cush.
Ellicott notes the king's name is Saul, and locates Rehoboth by the river near Nineveh on the Euphrates (cf. Genesis 10:11).
Clarke reports the Targum naming the place a great city on the banks of the Euphrates.

Ellicott settles the readings — Pau for Pai, Hadad for Hadar, ben Achbor added — and notes Mehetabel ('God benefits'), the only named queen of Edom, may have been an Israelite though her name is Aramean.
Clarke reads Mezahab as 'golden waters' and reports the Targum's legend of the diligent, then converted, Mehetabel who called silver and gold of no real worth.

outliers.note: Passes on the Targum's moralizing legend that Mehetabel, grown rich by diligence, on her conversion asked 'What is this gold?' — reported for its moral, not as history.

schools.summary: Ellicott reads the verse as the end of the monarchy — Hadad the last king, Edom then ruled by clan-chiefs (chiliarchs, heads of thousands) whose names are tribal and local, Timnah and Aholibamah being towns — and cites Ewald that David may have reduced Edom to tribal government.
Clarke gives the Targum that Hadad's kingdom ended, his land subdued and the dukes ruling from Gebal.

Ellicott derives the meanings of Shem, Ham, and Japheth from racial complexion, calls the following list an abridgment of the Table of Nations, and notes the old reckoning of seventy nations, the names being peoples not persons.
Henry arranges Noah's three lines by their bearing on the church — Japheth the strangers, Ham the enemies, Shem the allies — yet presses the one blood of all nations (Mal. 2:10).
Ellicott identifies Jetur with the Itureans of Luke 3:1 and owns the other names obscure; Keil notes the generations are taken from the older record and there explained.
Ellicott counts Midian's five clans, reckons Keturah's line at twelve tribes to match Ishmael's, and finds Ephah attested in the Assyrian records.
Ellicott takes the verse from Genesis 25:19, explains that Esau is named first because his line is enumerated first, and dwells on Israel as the more honourable name that supplanted Jacob.

outliers.note: Reports, without adopting, the modern theory that double names like Jacob-Israel and Esau-Edom preserve a trace of an ancient fusion of distinct races.

themes: the great fork of the chapter
the nation named before it is narrowed to

schools.summary: Ellicott defends the terse 'sons of Anah, Dishon' (Dishon a collective tribal name) and notes the omitted Aholibamah, daughter of Anah, who reappears among the chiefs at v.52; Keil keeps these clans as the Horite stock fused into Edom.

themes: the sons of Anah and Dishon

schools.summary: Ellicott reads Hadad's victory over Midian as a glimpse of kindred feuds, likens Edom's kings to Israel's judges raised by emergencies, and opens the polytheism of Edom through the name Hadad, the Syrian sun-god and giver of grapes.
Ellicott glosses Masrekah as the place of Sorek vines and keeps that these royal names are tribal and local; Keil places Samlah in his order among the kings.
Ellicott reads Baal-hanan ('Baal has been gracious') as, with Hadad, a plain token of the polytheism of ancient Edom; Keil sets him seventh in the succession.
Ellicott counts only eleven chiefs where the older record has twelve or thirteen, and judges a name has fallen out of the ancient text.
Wesley closes with a meditation on mortality — the multitudes who acted their parts and retired into darkness, the paths of death trodden paths.

themes: the line traced toward the King who does not die

schools.summary: Ellicott gives the sense of Ham (black, sunburnt) and keeps the rule that the names are peoples and countries.

Henry reads Ham's line with an eye on the church's enemies — Nimrod, the Egyptians of Mizraim, the Canaanites of Canaan — and sets God's love against them, giving Egypt for Israel's ransom (Isa. 43:3).
Wesley: the Jebusite, Amorite, and Girgashite are peoples not persons, all descended from Canaan, some afterward extinct or absorbed and heard of no more.
Henry sets these among the Canaanites Israel struggled to dispossess, recorded particularly because of their dealings with the church.
Ellicott keeps the rule that the sons of Shem are peoples as much as men, the nations of the nearer East.
Wesley notes the last four names are really sons of Aram — 'sons' taken to include grandsons, or 'the children of Aram' understood before Uz, per Genesis 10:23.
Henry names the eastern nations sprung from Shem — Assyrians, Syrians, Chaldeans, Persians, Arabians — while the promise runs quietly through Arphaxad.

themes: Arphaxad and the line of promise

schools.summary: Wesley weighs whether Arphaxad begat Shelah immediately or mediately through the Cainan inserted in Luke 3:35.
Henry marks the descent of the promise from Shem to Eber, in whom the Hebrew nation finds its name.
Wesley marks the deliberate turn — having surveyed the nations, the historian returns to a fuller genealogy of Shem, from whom the Jews descended.
Henry describes the register hastening to Abraham's line, breaking off from all Noah's families but Arphaxad's, from whom Christ was to come.
